agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with an established domain model governing Shipment-to-Carrier assignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments (>500 kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, without supervisor approval. This directly conflicts with two foundational invariants of the existing model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be delivered, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment. One Carrier per Shipment, locked after assignment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, only a Supervisor can change it. This is a system invariant."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"The only role authorized to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment. This is a domain-level access control rule, not a UI preference."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed behavior where the system autonomously creates multiple sub-shipments from a single shipment based on a weight threshold, each with a different carrier — bypassing supervisor authority."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The invariant guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. If a Shipment has zero or multiple Carriers, the system cannot determine responsibility for delivery — liability, tracking, SLAs, and billing all break. The system is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting against unauthorized changes to carrier responsibility. Without it, automated processes or lower-privilege users could silently redirect freight, creating liability gaps, audit failures, and potential fraud vectors.","testable_assertion":"Any state transition that changes a Shipment's assigned Carrier must have an authenticated Supervisor principal in the command context; otherwise the transition is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — a shipment physically leaves on one truck with one responsible carrier. This is the enforcement point of BR-1. If violated, you have freight with ambiguous custody, which is a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"The Dispatch command must fail if the Shipment's assigned Carrier is null or if more than one Carrier is assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A 501 kg shipment is auto-split into two sub-shipments. The parent Shipment now has no Carrier (or two). Who is responsible if the freight is lost in transit?","expected_behavior":"Under the current model this state is illegal. The system must reject it. There is no concept of shared or split carrier responsibility."},{"scenario":"Auto-split creates sub-shipments and assigns different carriers without supervisor approval. A carrier disputes the assignment.","expected_behavior":"The carrier is correct to dispute — the domain rule says only a Supervisor can make or change carrier assignments. An automated process lacks this authority."},{"scenario":"A 1500 kg shipment is split into three sub-shipments. One sub-shipment is delayed. The customer calls asking about 'their shipment' — which one is it?","expected_behavior":"The current model has no parent-child shipment relationship. There is no way to present a unified view. The customer experience degrades because the domain model cannot represent this."},{"scenario":"Auto-split fires, but only 2 of 3 sub-shipments find available carriers. The third has no carrier at dispatch time.","expected_behavior":"BR-3 is violated — a sub-shipment exists at dispatch with no carrier. The system must reject dispatch, but the parent shipment has already been dissolved."},{"scenario":"Shipment weighs exactly 500 kg.","expected_behavior":"Should NOT split (the rule says 'exceeds 500 kg'). Boundary must be strictly greater-than, not greater-than-or-equal."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments changes billing granularity — invoicing, insurance, and liability allocation all assume one carrier per shipment. Auto-split without accounting model changes will cause revenue leakage or billing disputes.","operational":"Severe. Introduces a new entity (sub-shipment) with no established lifecycle, no tracking integration, no SLA framework. Operations teams would need to manage N shipments where they previously managed one, with no tooling support.","user":"Negative. Customers expect to track one shipment. Splitting into sub-shipments without a unifying concept degrades visibility. Carriers receive assignments without supervisor vetting, eroding trust in the assignment process.","risk":"Critical. Two hard invariants are violated simultaneously: (1) one-carrier-per-shipment, and (2) supervisor-only reassignment. This is not a policy change — it is a structural contradiction with the existing domain model. Shipping without resolving this risks corrupt state, liability gaps, and audit failures.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is bypassed. They lose control over a process they are accountable for. Expect resistance and compliance concerns."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive automated assignments without supervisor review. May receive partial loads they did not bid on. Contract and SLA implications."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single shipment becomes multiple trackable entities. Visibility and communication degrade unless a parent-child tracking model is built."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier = 1 invoice line. Splitting creates billing complexity — partial shipments, split liability, pro-rated insurance."},{"group":"Compliance / Legal","impact":"Chain of custody is broken if carrier assignment bypasses supervisor authorization. Audit trail for who authorized each carrier becomes unclear."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the feature request as stated contains two direct contradictions with established invariants (BR-1 and BR-2). These are not ambiguities — they are structural conflicts. No acceptance criteria can be defined until the contradictions are resolved. The spec is not implementable as written.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing invariants are clear and well-defined","The feature request violates both BR-1 and BR-2","The concept of 'sub-shipment' does not exist in the current domain model"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants or introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup)","Whether 'without supervisor involvement' means fully automated or just reduced friction","What the weight threshold of 500 kg is based on — regulatory, operational, or arbitrary"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants are intentional and load-bearing, not accidental legacy constraints","The product team has not yet consulted with operations or legal about the implications"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment. This directly violates BR-1 (exactly one carrier per shipment). Even if sub-shipments are created, the system has no concept of a parent-child shipment relationship, and each sub-shipment would still need exactly one carrier — but the creation and assignment process itself bypasses the supervisor-only rule (BR-2).","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Supervisors","Carriers","Compliance / Legal","Customers"],"options":["OPTION A: Introduce a new aggregate — 'ShipmentGroup' — that contains multiple Shipments. Each child Shipment still has exactly one Carrier (BR-1 preserved). The ShipmentGroup is what the customer sees. Supervisor must approve the split and carrier assignments (BR-2 preserved). Auto-split proposes the split; Supervisor confirms.","OPTION B: Relax BR-2 to allow automated carrier assignment for system-initiated splits only, with post-hoc supervisor review. BR-1 is preserved via sub-shipments. This is a weaker guarantee but faster operationally.","OPTION C: Reject the feature as specified. The weight limit should be enforced at booking time — shipments over 500 kg are simply not accepted without pre-arranged multi-carrier coordination by a supervisor."],"recommendation":"OPTION A. It resolves the contradiction without violating either invariant. It introduces a new domain concept (ShipmentGroup) rather than breaking existing ones. The product team gets their split behavior; supervisors retain authority; carriers keep their 1:1 assignment guarantee. This requires real domain modeling work but protects system integrity."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"'Without requiring supervisor involvement' directly contradicts BR-2, which states only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier. Auto-assigning carriers to newly created sub-shipments IS carrier assignment — the fact that it is a new sub-shipment rather than a reassignment does not change the business reality that a carrier is being committed to freight without supervisor authorization.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Supervisors","Compliance / Legal"],"options":["Allow auto-split to propose assignments that require supervisor batch-approval (one click to approve all sub-assignments)","Create a new 'SystemSplit' authorization level that is auditable but does not require real-time supervisor action","Reject the bypass of supervisor authority entirely"],"recommendation":"Supervisor batch-approval. Reduces friction (product team's goal) while preserving the authorization invariant. The supervisor approves the split as a whole rather than each individual assignment."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single logical shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split assigns carriers without supervisor involvement","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can assign or reassign a Carrier to a Shipment"},{"violation":"Introduces 'sub-shipment' concept with no domain model support","business_rule_broken":"No aggregate or entity exists to represent parent-child shipment relationships. Implementing this without proper domain modeling creates orphaned entities with no lifecycle management."}]}

The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments (>500 kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, without supervisor approval. This directly conflicts with two foundational invariants of the existing model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[
